attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents with a three month history of shortness of breath on exertion and fatigue. Her past medical history is not significant. On examination her blood pressure is 130/80 mmHg, pulse 88/min, respiratory rate 24/min, and SpO2 92% on room air. Her JVP is elevated. Heart auscultation reveals a loud pulmonic component of the second heart sound (P2). Lung auscultation is unremarkable. She has mild, bilateral pedal oedema.

An ECG shows right axis deviation and an R wave in aVL ≥ 11 mm and an S wave in V3 ≥ 14 mm. An echocardiogram shows right ventricular hypertrophy and moderate right ventricular systolic dysfunction, and an elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP) of 75mmHg.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely underlying pathophysiological process?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the clinical scenario, the patient is most likely suffering from pulmonary arterial hypertension because of the ECG findings demonstrating right ventricular hypertrophy (R wave in aVL ≥ 11 mm (millimetres) in men or ≥ 10 mm in women, plus S wave in V3 ≥ 14 mm in men or ≥ 12 mm in women is the voltage criteria for right ventricular hypertrophy) and echo confirming the finding of right ventricular hypertrophy along with right ventricular systolic dysfunction and an elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP). Pulmonary hypertension has five classes where hypertension may be due to:

  • arterial disease (idiopathic, heritable, or drug-induced)

  • left heart disease

  • lung disease and/or hypoxia

  • chronic thromboembolic pulmonary disease

  • uncertain aetiology

Specifically, this patient most likely has pulmonary arterial hypertension given the absence of underlying lung or cardiac disease. Pulmonary arterial hypertension is a type of pulmonary vascular disease. Modern vasodilator therapies (eg Bosentan, Macitentan) have been shown to improve outcomes in these patients with PAH, which hitherto had a dire prognosis.

Obstructive lung disease, such as COPD, is characterised by airway obstruction, which leads to limitations in airflow, decreased oxygenation and retention of carbon dioxide. Patients with COPD may present with dyspnoea, wheezing, cough, and sputum production. Physical examination may reveal reduced breath sounds, wheezing, and prolonged expiration. In the case presented above the findings on examination and investigation suggest that obstructive lung disease is not the primary cause of the symptoms.

Left-sided heart failure is characterised by a decrease in cardiac output, which leads to pulmonary congestion and dyspnoea on exertion. Physical examination may reveal pulmonary crackles, elevated jugular venous pressure, and peripheral oedema. Echocardiography may show a reduced left ventricular ejection fraction.

Pulmonary embolism is a common cause of acute dyspnoea and can lead to pulmonary hypertension from thromboembolism (CTEPH). Patients with CTEPH do not present acutely, instead, they present with insidious onset of dyspnea, and signs of PH and have abnormal V/Q scans (which are more sensitive for CTEPH chronic changes than CTPA for acute PE).

Pericardial effusion is the accumulation of fluid in the pericardial sac, which can lead to pericardial tamponade and haemodynamic compromise. Patients with pericardial effusion may present with dyspnoea, chest pain, and signs of cardiac tamponade, such as hypotension, pulsus paradoxus, and elevated jugular venous pressure. Echocardiography is the primary diagnostic test for pericardial effusion and may show a pericardial fluid collection.
learningPoints: Pulmonary arterial hypertension is a type of pulmonary vascular disease characterised by elevated pulmonary artery systolic pressure (PASP).
references: Hassoun PM. Pulmonary Arterial Hypertension. N Engl J Med. 2021 Dec 16;385(25):2361-2376. doi: 10.1056/NEJMra2000348. PMID: 34910865.

Oldroyd SH, Manek G, Sankari A, et al. Pulmonary Hypertension. [Updated 2022 Dec 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2023 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK482463/
